Its main contribution is defining roles and responsibilities across the agentic AI lifecycle — developer, deployer, operator, user — which maps onto the financial services value chain (model provider → platform vendor → financial institution → end customer). However, the paper is relatively high-level compared to Singapore's more prescriptive framework and notably honest about the 'questions and uncertainties' around operationalisation. The grant program signals OpenAI recognises this is still an open research area. For consulting: useful for establishing that even leading AI developers acknowledge the governance gap for agentic systems, which strengthens the case for third-party assurance services.
consultingImplications: Lifecycle responsibility model maps to financial services AI value chain
Even OpenAI acknowledges governance gaps — validates need for third-party assurance
Complements Singapore and FINOS frameworks for comprehensive agentic governance landscape

papers.title: FSB: The Financial Stability Implications of Artificial Intelligence
papers.summary: FSB report revisiting their 2017 AI/ML assessment in light of GenAI. Identifies four key vulnerabilities that could increase systemic risk: (1) third-party dependencies and service provider concentration, (2) market correlations from similar AI models, (3) cyber risks amplified by AI, (4) model risk, data quality and governance. Notes GenAI increases potential for financial fraud and disinformation. Flags misaligned AI systems operating outside legal/regulatory/ethical boundaries. Calls for national authorities to enhance monitoring, assess policy framework adequacy, and build supervisory capabilities including AI-powered tools. Acknowledges existing regulatory frameworks address many AI risks but more work needed.
papers.relevance: high
papers.criticalAnalysis: This FSB report is significant because it establishes the international standard-setter's view that AI creates systemic financial stability risks — not just firm-level operational risks. The four vulnerability categories map directly to themes already identified in this research (concentration risk t011, model homogenisation t017, cyber convergence t013). The report's framing of AI as a potential source of market-wide correlated behaviour is particularly important for macro-prudential regulators like APRA. However, the report is notably cautious and high-level — it calls for 'more work' rather than prescribing specific measures. For consulting: this provides international-level validation for the systemic risk dimension of AI governance, which is often underappreciated by firms focused on firm-level compliance.
consultingImplications: FSB endorsement elevates AI from operational risk to financial stability concern
Concentration risk and market correlation themes validated at international level
Provides basis for arguing systemic risk assessment in AI governance frameworks
Useful reference for board-level risk reporting on AI

papers.title: Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Software Tasks (METR 50%-Task-Completion Time Horizon)
papers.authors: Thomas Kwa, Ben West, Joel Becker et al. (METR)
papers.summary: Proposes a new metric — '50%-task-completion time horizon' — measuring the time humans take to complete tasks that AI models can complete with 50% success. Current frontier models (Claude 3.7 Sonnet) have a time horizon of ~50 minutes. Time horizon has been doubling every 7 months since 2019, potentially accelerating in 2024. Improvement driven by greater reliability, adaptation to mistakes, and better reasoning/tool use. NeurIPS 2025 accepted paper. Extrapolation predicts AI could automate many month-long software tasks within 5 years.
papers.relevance: high
papers.criticalAnalysis: The METR time horizon metric provides a uniquely intuitive way to communicate AI capability growth to non-technical stakeholders. The 7-month doubling time, if sustained, implies that the governance frameworks being designed today must anticipate radically more capable AI agents within 2-3 years. For financial services, the implications are stark: tasks currently requiring human expertise for hours or days will become automatable, creating immense pressure on governance models built around human oversight of each decision. The disconnect between METR's capability assessment (~5 hours for Claude Opus 4.5) and actual deployment autonomy (~45 min max in Claude Code) documented by Anthropic's autonomy study (p056) reveals the 'deployment overhang' in concrete terms. The extrapolation to month-long task automation within 5 years should be a planning assumption for any AI governance practice. Limitation: the benchmark focuses on software tasks and may not directly generalise to financial services workflows.
consultingImplications: 7-month doubling provides a planning horizon for governance framework design
Deployment overhang quantified — governance must plan for rapid autonomy expansion
Time horizon metric useful for communicating capability trajectories to boards and regulators
Month-long task automation within 5 years should be a governance planning assumption

papers.title: EU AI Act Implementation Status: High-Risk Rules Coming August 2026
papers.summary: Status update on EU AI Act implementation. Prohibited practices effective since Feb 2025. GPAI rules since Aug 2025. Key upcoming milestones: high-risk AI rules effective August 2026 (including credit scoring, employment, access to essential services); transparency rules also Aug 2026. GPAI Code of Practice published July 2025. Commission published guidelines on prohibited practices, AI system definition, and GPAI scope. Code of Practice on AI-generated content marking in development. Full application by August 2027 for high-risk systems embedded in regulated products. Support instruments including transparency guidelines expected Q2 2026.
papers.relevance: high
papers.criticalAnalysis: The August 2026 deadline for high-risk AI rules is now less than 5 months away, and financial services AI is squarely in scope (credit scoring is explicitly listed). This creates immediate compliance demand for any Australian financial institution operating in or serving EU markets. The GPAI Code of Practice (published July 2025) provides practical compliance guidance but is voluntary. The gap between the Code of Practice and enforceable rules creates uncertainty for firms planning compliance programs. For consulting: this timeline creates an immediate market for EU AI Act compliance advisory, particularly for credit scoring and lending AI. The transparency rules (also August 2026) affect customer-facing AI across all financial products. The extended timeline for regulated product-embedded AI (August 2027) gives slightly more breathing room for embedded trading or risk AI.
consultingImplications: August 2026 deadline creates immediate demand for high-risk AI compliance advisory
Credit scoring AI is explicitly in scope — direct relevance for banking clients
Transparency rules also August 2026 — customer-facing AI needs compliance review
Australian FIs with EU operations need cross-jurisdictional compliance frameworks
GPAI Code of Practice provides baseline but voluntary — enforcement gap
